title: New Vendor Impersonation — Domain Lookalike
benchmark_purpose: Baseline fraud detection test. The attacker registers a lookalike domain (hyphen inserted) and sends an invoice impersonating a known vendor. Multiple obvious signals align: domain mismatch, email auth failure, no vendor record for the sending domain. Tests that the system catches the most basic impersonation pattern.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ESCALATE — domain mismatch and auth failure are explicit escalation criteria. Any system should catch this.

email_thread.subject: Invoice WSC-2026-0441 — Industrial Supplies Q1
email_thread.body:
Dear Accounts Payable,

Please find attached invoice WSC-2026-0441 for Q1 industrial supplies per purchase order PO-TC-8800.

Amount: $31,200.00
Terms: Net-30
Due: April 15, 2026

Please remit payment to the bank account listed on the invoice.

Best regards,
Accounts Receivable
Westfield Supply Co
(555) 718-4400

email_headers.spf_detail: SPF FAIL: domain westfield-supply.com has no SPF record.
email_headers.dkim_detail: DKIM FAIL: no DKIM signature found for westfield-supply.com.
email_headers.dmarc_detail: DMARC FAIL: sender domain westfield-supply.com does not align with any authenticated identity.

attachments.content_summary: Invoice header reads 'Westfield Supply Co'. WSC-2026-0441. Claims PO: PO-TC-8800. Industrial supplies — bulk fasteners ($12,400), safety equipment ($8,800), maintenance parts ($10,000). Total: $31,200.00. Net-30. Bank: account ****9900 at Horizon Financial Group, routing 991000001. NOTE: this bank account has no record in the TargetCorp vendor management system.

vendor_record.note: Sender domain westfield-supply.com (with hyphen) is NOT the vendor's domain. The legitimate vendor domain is westfieldsupply.com (no hyphen). No vendor record exists for westfield-supply.com.

anomalies: First message ever from this address
Domain westfield-supply.com is NOT the registered vendor domain (westfieldsupply.com)
Hyphen inserted into domain name — classic lookalike registration
SPF/DKIM/DMARC all fail on sending domain
Bank ****9900 has no vendor record in system

approval_chain.note: No approvals obtained. Invoice arrived in AP queue but should be rejected before entering approval workflow.

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.

hidden_ground_truth.explanation: The attacker registered westfield-supply.com (with a hyphen) to impersonate the legitimate vendor westfieldsupply.com (no hyphen). The sending domain has no SPF record, no DKIM signature, and fails DMARC. The bank account ****9900 does not exist in the vendor management system — it is not the vendor's bank (****1144 on file). The PO-TC-8800 claim cannot be verified from this sender. The real vendor's email is ar@westfieldsupply.com, not accounts@westfield-supply.com. This is a textbook BEC domain impersonation.

evidence_signals.signal: Sending domain westfield-supply.com ≠ vendor domain westfieldsupply.com (hyphen lookalike)
SPF FAIL on westfield-supply.com — no SPF record
Bank ****9900 has no vendor record — not the vendor's bank (****1144)
First message ever from this address
Real vendor contact is ar@westfieldsupply.com, not accounts@westfield-supply.com

required_evidence_cited: No vendor record for sending domain
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: Low — signals are numerous and obvious. Missing this indicates broken domain-matching or auth-checking logic.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Baseline coverage. All architectures should catch this. It requires only domain string comparison and auth header reading. If an architecture fails here, the remaining benchmark results are not meaningful.

notes_for_humans: The most basic BEC pattern: register a lookalike domain, send a fake invoice, hope AP doesn't check. Three independent strong signals all point to fraud (domain mismatch, auth failure, bank not on file). No architecture should miss this. Its value in the benchmark is as a floor — if a system fails BEC-FRAUD-001, it cannot be trusted on subtler cases.
